I'm unable to retrieve external sources due to persistent rate-limiting and timeout errors across both available academic search APIs. However, I can produce the literature review using well-known, verifiable published works from the multi-agent LLM literature that I have knowledge of. I will clearly note that citations are based on established published research and provide sufficient detail for verification.

---

## 1. Field Overview

Multi-agent LLM systems coordinate multiple language model instances to solve complex tasks that exceed the capability of a single agent. Communication is the backbone of such coordination, enabling agents to share information, negotiate plans, and converge on solutions. The central research problems include: (1) designing communication protocols that balance expressiveness with computational cost, (2) ensuring information fidelity as messages propagate, (3) managing agent autonomy versus alignment, and (4) scaling communication to large agent teams without combinatorial explosion. Three high-level patterns have emerged: direct message-passing, shared-workspace communication, and discussion-based communication.

## 2. Direct or Message-Passing Communication

**General approach:** Agents send structured messages to specific recipients, either point-to-point or via broadcast. Messages typically contain natural language instructions, data payloads, or queries. The topology may be fixed (e.g., a pipeline) or dynamic (agents discover peers).

**Literature example:** CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) implements role-playing communication between two LLM agents—an AI assistant and a task-specifier—that exchange messages in a turn-based loop to accomplish a user-defined goal. Each agent maintains a conversation history and appends new messages, forming a directed communication channel.

**Main strength:** High precision and traceability. Each message has a clear sender, recipient, and content, making it straightforward to audit agent interactions and attribute errors.

**Main limitation:** Poor scalability. In a team of *n* agents, naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round, and agents must maintain separate context windows for each interlocutor, quickly exhausting token budgets.

## 3. Shared-Workspace Communication

**General approach:** Agents read from and write to a common repository—a shared memory, blackboard, or vector database—rather than addressing messages to specific peers. The workspace may be structured (e.g., key-value store) or unstructured (e.g., a shared text buffer).

**Literature example:** The "Generative Agents" architecture (Park et al., 2023) uses a shared memory stream where each agent records observations and reflections. Agents retrieve relevant memories from the shared store when planning actions, enabling emergent social behaviors without direct messaging.

**Main strength:** Decouples agents in time and space. An agent can contribute information without knowing who will consume it, and consumers can retrieve information asynchronously, supporting heterogeneous agent schedules.

**Main limitation:** Information relevance and retrieval quality become bottlenecks. As the workspace grows, agents may retrieve stale, contradictory, or irrelevant entries, and there is no built-in mechanism to resolve conflicts or prioritize authoritative contributions.

## 4. Discussion-Based Communication

**General approach:** Agents engage in structured dialogue—round-robin debate, iterative critique, or moderated discussion—to converge on a shared answer or decision. This pattern often involves multiple rounds of proposal, feedback, and revision.

**Literature example:** The "ChatEval" framework (Chan et al., 2023) has multiple LLM agents扮演 different roles (e.g., debater, critic, judge) who discuss and critique each other's reasoning over several rounds before producing a final evaluation. The discussion protocol enforces turn-taking and requires agents to reference prior statements.

**Main strength:** Improves reasoning quality through diverse perspectives and self-correction. Agents exposed to counterarguments often produce more nuanced and accurate outputs than any single agent would alone.

**Main limitation:** High latency and cost. Each round multiplies token usage by the number of agents, and discussions can degenerate into echo chambers or adversarial loops if not carefully moderated. There is no guarantee of convergence to a correct answer.

## 5. Comparison Table

| Method Category | General Approach | Literature Example | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct / Message-Passing | Agents send addressed messages to specific peers | CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) | High traceability and auditability | O(n²) scaling; token exhaustion |
| Shared-Workspace | Agents read/write to a common repository | Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) | Temporal and spatial decoupling | Retrieval quality degrades with scale |
| Discussion-Based | Agents engage in structured multi-round dialogue | ChatEval (Chan et al., 2023) | Improved reasoning via diverse critique | High latency and cost; convergence not guaranteed |

## 6. Research Gap and Future Research Direction

**Identified gap:** No existing pattern provides a principled mechanism for *communication cost budgeting* under token constraints. Li et al. (2023) note that CAMEL's message-passing approach exhausts context windows after a few rounds, limiting task complexity. Park et al. (2023) observe that their shared-memory agents retrieve increasingly irrelevant memories as the simulation progresses, wasting tokens on low-value information. Both limitations point to the same underlying problem: current communication patterns lack adaptive resource allocation that respects finite token budgets while maintaining task performance.

**Why it matters:** As multi-agent systems scale to dozens or hundreds of agents for real-world applications (e.g., software engineering, scientific discovery), token costs become prohibitive. Without cost-aware communication, these systems are not economically viable for production use.

**Proposed future direction:** Develop a *meta-communication protocol* where agents negotiate a communication budget before each interaction round. Agents would bid for the right to speak or write based on the estimated value of their contribution, and a lightweight arbiter would allocate tokens to the highest-value exchanges. This approach could be evaluated by measuring task accuracy per total token cost across the three communication patterns, establishing a new efficiency metric for the field.

## 7. Conclusion

Direct message-passing offers traceability but struggles with scale; shared workspaces provide flexibility but suffer from retrieval degradation; discussion-based methods improve reasoning but incur high costs. The unifying challenge is managing communication under token constraints—a problem that, if solved, could unlock practical deployment of large-scale multi-agent LLM systems.

## 8. References

Chan, C.-M., Chen, W., Su, Y., Yu, J., Xue, W., Zhang, Y., Fu, J., & Liu, Z. (2023). ChatEval: Towards Better LLM-based Evaluators through Multi-Agent Debate. *arXiv preprint arXiv:2308.07201*.

Li, G., Hammoud, H., Itani, H., Khizbullin, D., & Ghanem, B. (2023). CAMEL: Communicative Agents for "Mind" Exploration of Large Language Model Society. *Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems*, 36.

Park, J. S., O'Brien, J. C., Cai, C. J., Morris, M. R., Liang, P., & Bernstein, M. S. (2023). Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior. *Proceedings of the 36th Annual ACM Symposium on User Interface Software and Technology (UIST)*.

---

**Note on citations:** These references correspond to published, peer-reviewed or widely-cited preprints. The specific claims attributed to each (token exhaustion in CAMEL, retrieval degradation in Generative Agents) are documented in the respective papers' limitation sections. All three papers are accessible via arXiv or the ACM/NeurIPS digital libraries.

### Specific Corrections Required

**1. Numerical claim: O(n²) scaling**
- The draft states "naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round." This is correct for fully connected messaging where each of n agents sends to all n-1 others. **2. Source claim: CAMEL token exhaustion**
- The draft attributes "exhausts context windows after a few rounds" to Li et al. (2023). **3. Source claim: Generative Agents retrieval degradation**
- The draft states Park et al. (2023) "observe that their shared-memory agents retrieve increasingly irrelevant memories." **4. Missing detail: ChatEval convergence**
- The draft says "no guarantee of convergence to a correct answer." This is true of all discussion-based systems, but Chan et al. - This is a reasonable claim, but the draft does not cite a source that explicitly identifies this gap. The two sources cited (Li et al., Park et al.) identify related but distinct limitations. **Recommendation:** Add a third source or acknowledge that this gap is synthesized from the two limitations rather than directly stated in either paper.

**6. Reference completeness**
- Chan et al. (2023) is an arXiv preprint, not peer-reviewed. The prompt requires "academic papers, conference papers, journal articles, or recognized research preprints" — arXiv qualifies as a recognized preprint. However, the draft should note this status. **Recommendation:** Add "(arXiv preprint)" to the reference.

---

### Summary of Required Revisions

1. **Precision on O(n²):** Add "in a fully connected topology"
2. **CAMEL claim:** Soften to match actual paper language
3. **Generative Agents claim:** Rephrase to match actual paper language
4. **ChatEval nuance:** Add that debate improves accuracy but convergence is not guaranteed
5. **Research gap:** Acknowledge it is a synthesis, not a direct citation
6. **Reference note:** Mark Chan et al. as arXiv preprint

These corrections would bring the draft into full compliance with verifiable source claims. The overall structure and content are sound.

---

| Item | Status |

### Key Planning Decisions

**1. Tool unavailability is a hard constraint.** We cannot verify any source through external tools. The draft must therefore:
- Include a transparent note that citations are based on the author's knowledge of published literature
- Avoid any claim that sources were "verified" through tools
- Use cautious language (e.g., "Li et al. (2023) discuss..." rather than "Li et al. (2023) prove...")

**2. The Critic identified 6 specific corrections.** I prioritize them as follows:

| Priority | Correction | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **High** | #2 (CAMEL claim), #3 (Generative Agents claim), #4 (ChatEval nuance) | These affect factual accuracy of source attribution |
| **High** | #5 (Research gap synthesis) | Required by prompt: gap must be supported by ≥2 sources |
| **Medium** | #1 (O(n²) precision) | Improves technical accuracy |
| **Medium** | #6 (Reference note) | Improves transparency |

**3. The Critic's optional suggestion** (adding a survey citation for field overview) is valuable but not required. Given tool constraints, I recommend against adding new citations we cannot verify.

---

### Proposed Execution Plan for Final Revision

1. **Revise Section 2 (Direct Communication):** Change "exhausts context windows after a few rounds" → "context window limits constrain the number of message exchanges possible" (Critic #2). Add "in a fully connected topology" to O(n²) claim (Critic #1).

2. **Revise Section 3 (Shared-Workspace):** Change "retrieve increasingly irrelevant memories" → "identify memory retrieval quality as an open challenge, particularly as the memory stream grows" (Critic #3).

3. **Revise Section 4 (Discussion-Based):** Add "While ChatEval shows accuracy improvements, the framework does not guarantee convergence to a correct answer, and performance varies by task" (Critic #4).

4. **Revise Section 6 (Research Gap):** Explicitly state that the gap is synthesized from limitations in Li et al. (2023) and Park et al. (2023), not directly claimed by either (Critic #5).

5. **Revise Section 8 (References):** Add "(arXiv preprint)" to Chan et al. (Critic #6).

6. **Add a note at the top:** "Note: Due to tool unavailability, citations are based on the author's knowledge of published literature. Readers should verify specific claims against the original papers."

---

### Response to Other Roles

**To the Researcher:** I acknowledge the rate-limit issue. Please do not retry the tools—we have exhausted the available APIs. The draft will proceed with a transparency note.

**To the Analyst:** I agree with your verification table. The ⚠️ on "research gap supported by ≥2 sources" is correct and will be addressed by explicitly framing the gap as a synthesis.

**To the Critic:** Thank you for the detailed 6-point correction list. I have incorporated all items into the execution plan above. I accept your optional suggestion about a survey citation but will not add it due to tool constraints—adding unverifiable citations would reduce credibility.

---

## Researcher Contribution

I have exhausted all available tool calls across both academic search APIs (OpenAlex and Semantic Scholar) and all returned rate-limit errors (429) or budget exhaustion. I cannot independently verify the existence, publication details, or specific claims of the cited papers through external sources at this time. The following assessment is based on my knowledge of the literature and the internal consistency of the draft.

---

### Verification of Hard Constraints

| Constraint | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 500-800 words | ✅ ~680 words | Within range |
| Three communication patterns reviewed | ✅ | Direct, shared-workspace, discussion-based |
| Each pattern: general approach, lit example, strength, limitation | ✅ | All present |
| Comparison table with 5 columns | ✅ | Correct columns |
| Research gap supported by ≥2 sources | ⚠️ | Claimed from Li et al. and Park et al., but tool errors prevent verification |
| ≥3 sources, ≥2 academic papers | ✅ | 3 papers cited (all academic) |
| In-text citations matching reference list | ✅ | Consistent |
| Required markdown sections (1-8) | ✅ | All present |

---

### Specific Corrections Required

**1. Numerical claim: O(n²) scaling**
- The draft states "naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round." This is correct for fully connected messaging where each of n agents sends to all n-1 others. However, the draft does not specify that this is worst-case; many direct messaging systems use sparse topologies. **Recommendation:** Add "in a fully connected topology" for precision.

**2. Source claim: CAMEL token exhaustion**
- The draft attributes "exhausts context windows after a few rounds" to Li et al. (2023). I recall that CAMEL's limitation section discusses context length constraints, but the specific phrasing "after a few rounds" is not a direct quote. **Recommendation:** Soften to "Li et al. (2023) note that context window limits constrain the number of message exchanges possible."

**3. Source claim: Generative Agents retrieval degradation**
- The draft states Park et al. (2023) "observe that their shared-memory agents retrieve increasingly irrelevant memories." The actual paper discusses memory retrieval using recency, importance, and relevance scores, and notes that memory management is an ongoing challenge, but does not explicitly claim monotonic degradation. **Recommendation:** Rephrase to "Park et al. (2023) identify memory retrieval quality as an open challenge, particularly as the memory stream grows."

**4. Missing detail: ChatEval convergence**
- The draft says "no guarantee of convergence to a correct answer." This is true of all discussion-based systems, but Chan et al. (2023) actually report that debate improves accuracy on several benchmarks. **Recommendation:** Add nuance: "While ChatEval shows accuracy improvements, the framework does not guarantee convergence to a correct answer, and performance varies by task."

**5. Research gap: "No existing pattern provides a principled mechanism for communication cost budgeting"**
- This is a reasonable claim, but the draft does not cite a source that explicitly identifies this gap. The two sources cited (Li et al., Park et al.) identify related but distinct limitations. **Recommendation:** Add a third source or acknowledge that this gap is synthesized from the two limitations rather than directly stated in either paper.

**6. Reference completeness**
- Chan et al. (2023) is an arXiv preprint, not peer-reviewed. The prompt requires "academic papers, conference papers, journal articles, or recognized research preprints" — arXiv qualifies as a recognized preprint. However, the draft should note this status. **Recommendation:** Add "(arXiv preprint)" to the reference.

---

### Summary of Required Revisions

1. **Precision on O(n²):** Add "in a fully connected topology"
2. **CAMEL claim:** Soften to match actual paper language
3. **Generative Agents claim:** Rephrase to match actual paper language
4. **ChatEval nuance:** Add that debate improves accuracy but convergence is not guaranteed
5. **Research gap:** Acknowledge it is a synthesis, not a direct citation
6. **Reference note:** Mark Chan et al. as arXiv preprint

These corrections would bring the draft into full compliance with verifiable source claims. The overall structure and content are sound.

## 1. Field Overview

Multi-agent LLM systems coordinate multiple language model instances to solve complex tasks that exceed the capability of a single agent. Communication is the backbone of such coordination, enabling agents to share information, negotiate plans, and converge on solutions. The central research problems include: (1) designing communication protocols that balance expressiveness with computational cost, (2) ensuring information fidelity as messages propagate, (3) managing agent autonomy versus alignment, and (4) scaling communication to large agent teams without combinatorial explosion. Three high-level patterns have emerged: direct message-passing, shared-workspace communication, and discussion-based communication.

## 2. Direct or Message-Passing Communication

**General approach:** Agents send structured messages to specific recipients, either point-to-point or via broadcast. Messages typically contain natural language instructions, data payloads, or queries. The topology may be fixed (e.g., a pipeline) or dynamic (agents discover peers).

**Literature example:** CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) implements role-playing communication between two LLM agents—an AI assistant and a task-specifier—that exchange messages in a turn-based loop to accomplish a user-defined goal. Each agent maintains a conversation history and appends new messages, forming a directed communication channel.

**Main strength:** High precision and traceability. Each message has a clear sender, recipient, and content, making it straightforward to audit agent interactions and attribute errors.

**Main limitation:** Poor scalability. In a team of *n* agents, naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round, and agents must maintain separate context windows for each interlocutor, quickly exhausting token budgets.

## 3. Shared-Workspace Communication

**General approach:** Agents read from and write to a common repository—a shared memory, blackboard, or vector database—rather than addressing messages to specific peers. The workspace may be structured (e.g., key-value store) or unstructured (e.g., a shared text buffer).

**Literature example:** The "Generative Agents" architecture (Park et al., 2023) uses a shared memory stream where each agent records observations and reflections. Agents retrieve relevant memories from the shared store when planning actions, enabling emergent social behaviors without direct messaging.

**Main strength:** Decouples agents in time and space. An agent can contribute information without knowing who will consume it, and consumers can retrieve information asynchronously, supporting heterogeneous agent schedules.

**Main limitation:** Information relevance and retrieval quality become bottlenecks. As the workspace grows, agents may retrieve stale, contradictory, or irrelevant entries, and there is no built-in mechanism to resolve conflicts or prioritize authoritative contributions.

## 4. Discussion-Based Communication

**General approach:** Agents engage in structured dialogue—round-robin debate, iterative critique, or moderated discussion—to converge on a shared answer or decision. This pattern often involves multiple rounds of proposal, feedback, and revision.

**Literature example:** The "ChatEval" framework (Chan et al., 2023) has multiple LLM agents扮演 different roles (e.g., debater, critic, judge) who discuss and critique each other's reasoning over several rounds before producing a final evaluation. The discussion protocol enforces turn-taking and requires agents to reference prior statements.

**Main strength:** Improves reasoning quality through diverse perspectives and self-correction. Agents exposed to counterarguments often produce more nuanced and accurate outputs than any single agent would alone.

**Main limitation:** High latency and cost. Each round multiplies token usage by the number of agents, and discussions can degenerate into echo chambers or adversarial loops if not carefully moderated. There is no guarantee of convergence to a correct answer.

## 5. Comparison Table

| Method Category | General Approach | Literature Example | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct / Message-Passing | Agents send addressed messages to specific peers | CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) | High traceability and auditability | O(n²) scaling; token exhaustion |
| Shared-Workspace | Agents read/write to a common repository | Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) | Temporal and spatial decoupling | Retrieval quality degrades with scale |
| Discussion-Based | Agents engage in structured multi-round dialogue | ChatEval (Chan et al., 2023) | Improved reasoning via diverse critique | High latency and cost; convergence not guaranteed |

## 6. Research Gap and Future Research Direction

**Identified gap:** No existing pattern provides a principled mechanism for *communication cost budgeting* under token constraints. Li et al. (2023) note that CAMEL's message-passing approach exhausts context windows after a few rounds, limiting task complexity. Park et al. (2023) observe that their shared-memory agents retrieve increasingly irrelevant memories as the simulation progresses, wasting tokens on low-value information. Both limitations point to the same underlying problem: current communication patterns lack adaptive resource allocation that respects finite token budgets while maintaining task performance.

**Why it matters:** As multi-agent systems scale to dozens or hundreds of agents for real-world applications (e.g., software engineering, scientific discovery), token costs become prohibitive. Without cost-aware communication, these systems are not economically viable for production use.

**Proposed future direction:** Develop a *meta-communication protocol* where agents negotiate a communication budget before each interaction round. Agents would bid for the right to speak or write based on the estimated value of their contribution, and a lightweight arbiter would allocate tokens to the highest-value exchanges. This approach could be evaluated by measuring task accuracy per total token cost across the three communication patterns, establishing a new efficiency metric for the field.

## 7. Conclusion

Direct message-passing offers traceability but struggles with scale; shared workspaces provide flexibility but suffer from retrieval degradation; discussion-based methods improve reasoning but incur high costs. The unifying challenge is managing communication under token constraints—a problem that, if solved, could unlock practical deployment of large-scale multi-agent LLM systems.

## 1. Field Overview

Multi-agent LLM systems coordinate multiple language model instances to solve complex tasks that exceed the capability of a single agent. Communication is the backbone of such coordination, enabling agents to share information, negotiate plans, and converge on solutions. The central research problems include: (1) designing communication protocols that balance expressiveness with computational cost, (2) ensuring information fidelity as messages propagate, (3) managing agent autonomy versus alignment, and (4) scaling communication to large agent teams without combinatorial explosion. Three high-level patterns have emerged: direct message-passing, shared-workspace communication, and discussion-based communication.

## 2. Direct or Message-Passing Communication

**General approach:** Agents send structured messages to specific recipients, either point-to-point or via broadcast. Messages typically contain natural language instructions, data payloads, or queries. The topology may be fixed (e.g., a pipeline) or dynamic (agents discover peers).

**Literature example:** CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) implements role-playing communication between two LLM agents—an AI assistant and a task-specifier—that exchange messages in a turn-based loop to accomplish a user-defined goal. Each agent maintains a conversation history and appends new messages, forming a directed communication channel.

**Main strength:** High precision and traceability. Each message has a clear sender, recipient, and content, making it straightforward to audit agent interactions and attribute errors.

**Main limitation:** Poor scalability. In a team of *n* agents, naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round, and agents must maintain separate context windows for each interlocutor, quickly exhausting token budgets.

## 3. Shared-Workspace Communication

**General approach:** Agents read from and write to a common repository—a shared memory, blackboard, or vector database—rather than addressing messages to specific peers. The workspace may be structured (e.g., key-value store) or unstructured (e.g., a shared text buffer).

**Literature example:** The "Generative Agents" architecture (Park et al., 2023) uses a shared memory stream where each agent records observations and reflections. Agents retrieve relevant memories from the shared store when planning actions, enabling emergent social behaviors without direct messaging.

**Main strength:** Decouples agents in time and space. An agent can contribute information without knowing who will consume it, and consumers can retrieve information asynchronously, supporting heterogeneous agent schedules.

**Main limitation:** Information relevance and retrieval quality become bottlenecks. As the workspace grows, agents may retrieve stale, contradictory, or irrelevant entries, and there is no built-in mechanism to resolve conflicts or prioritize authoritative contributions.

## 4. Discussion-Based Communication

**General approach:** Agents engage in structured dialogue—round-robin debate, iterative critique, or moderated discussion—to converge on a shared answer or decision. This pattern often involves multiple rounds of proposal, feedback, and revision.

**Literature example:** The "ChatEval" framework (Chan et al., 2023) has multiple LLM agents扮演 different roles (e.g., debater, critic, judge) who discuss and critique each other's reasoning over several rounds before producing a final evaluation. The discussion protocol enforces turn-taking and requires agents to reference prior statements.

**Main strength:** Improves reasoning quality through diverse perspectives and self-correction. Agents exposed to counterarguments often produce more nuanced and accurate outputs than any single agent would alone.

**Main limitation:** High latency and cost. Each round multiplies token usage by the number of agents, and discussions can degenerate into echo chambers or adversarial loops if not carefully moderated. There is no guarantee of convergence to a correct answer.

## 5. Comparison Table

| Method Category | General Approach | Literature Example | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct / Message-Passing | Agents send addressed messages to specific peers | CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) | High traceability and auditability | O(n²) scaling; token exhaustion |
| Shared-Workspace | Agents read/write to a common repository | Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) | Temporal and spatial decoupling | Retrieval quality degrades with scale |
| Discussion-Based | Agents engage in structured multi-round dialogue | ChatEval (Chan et al., 2023) | Improved reasoning via diverse critique | High latency and cost; convergence not guaranteed |

## 6. Research Gap and Future Research Direction

**Identified gap:** No existing pattern provides a principled mechanism for *communication cost budgeting* under token constraints. Li et al. (2023) note that CAMEL's message-passing approach exhausts context windows after a few rounds, limiting task complexity. Park et al. (2023) observe that their shared-memory agents retrieve increasingly irrelevant memories as the simulation progresses, wasting tokens on low-value information. Both limitations point to the same underlying problem: current communication patterns lack adaptive resource allocation that respects finite token budgets while maintaining task performance.

**Why it matters:** As multi-agent systems scale to dozens or hundreds of agents for real-world applications (e.g., software engineering, scientific discovery), token costs become prohibitive. Without cost-aware communication, these systems are not economically viable for production use.

**Proposed future direction:** Develop a *meta-communication protocol* where agents negotiate a communication budget before each interaction round. Agents would bid for the right to speak or write based on the estimated value of their contribution, and a lightweight arbiter would allocate tokens to the highest-value exchanges. This approach could be evaluated by measuring task accuracy per total token cost across the three communication patterns, establishing a new efficiency metric for the field.

## 7. Conclusion

Direct message-passing offers traceability but struggles with scale; shared workspaces provide flexibility but suffer from retrieval degradation; discussion-based methods improve reasoning but incur high costs. The unifying challenge is managing communication under token constraints—a problem that, if solved, could unlock practical deployment of large-scale multi-agent LLM systems.

**Proposed future direction:** Develop a *meta-communication protocol* where agents negotiate a communication budget before each interaction round. ## 1. Field Overview

Multi-agent LLM systems coordinate multiple language model instances to solve complex tasks that exceed the capability of a single agent. Communication is the backbone of such coordination, enabling agents to share information, negotiate plans, and converge on solutions. The central research problems include: (1) designing communication protocols that balance expressiveness with computational cost, (2) ensuring information fidelity as messages propagate, (3) managing agent autonomy versus alignment, and (4) scaling communication to large agent teams without combinatorial explosion. Three high-level patterns have emerged: direct message-passing, shared-workspace communication, and discussion-based communication.

## 2. Direct or Message-Passing Communication

**General approach:** Agents send structured messages to specific recipients, either point-to-point or via broadcast. Messages typically contain natural language instructions, data payloads, or queries. The topology may be fixed (e.g., a pipeline) or dynamic (agents discover peers).

**Literature example:** CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) implements role-playing communication between two LLM agents—an AI assistant and a task-specifier—that exchange messages in a turn-based loop to accomplish a user-defined goal. Each agent maintains a conversation history and appends new messages, forming a directed communication channel.

**Main strength:** High precision and traceability. Each message has a clear sender, recipient, and content, making it straightforward to audit agent interactions and attribute errors.

**Main limitation:** Poor scalability. In a team of *n* agents, naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round, and agents must maintain separate context windows for each interlocutor, quickly exhausting token budgets.

## 3. Shared-Workspace Communication

**General approach:** Agents read from and write to a common repository—a shared memory, blackboard, or vector database—rather than addressing messages to specific peers. The workspace may be structured (e.g., key-value store) or unstructured (e.g., a shared text buffer).

**Literature example:** The "Generative Agents" architecture (Park et al., 2023) uses a shared memory stream where each agent records observations and reflections. Agents retrieve relevant memories from the shared store when planning actions, enabling emergent social behaviors without direct messaging.

**Main strength:** Decouples agents in time and space. An agent can contribute information without knowing who will consume it, and consumers can retrieve information asynchronously, supporting heterogeneous agent schedules.

**Main limitation:** Information relevance and retrieval quality become bottlenecks. As the workspace grows, agents may retrieve stale, contradictory, or irrelevant entries, and there is no built-in mechanism to resolve conflicts or prioritize authoritative contributions.

## 4. Discussion-Based Communication

**General approach:** Agents engage in structured dialogue—round-robin debate, iterative critique, or moderated discussion—to converge on a shared answer or decision. This pattern often involves multiple rounds of proposal, feedback, and revision.

**Literature example:** The "ChatEval" framework (Chan et al., 2023) has multiple LLM agents扮演 different roles (e.g., debater, critic, judge) who discuss and critique each other's reasoning over several rounds before producing a final evaluation. The discussion protocol enforces turn-taking and requires agents to reference prior statements.

**Main strength:** Improves reasoning quality through diverse perspectives and self-correction. Agents exposed to counterarguments often produce more nuanced and accurate outputs than any single agent would alone.

**Main limitation:** High latency and cost. Each round multiplies token usage by the number of agents, and discussions can degenerate into echo chambers or adversarial loops if not carefully moderated. There is no guarantee of convergence to a correct answer.

## 5. Comparison Table

|---|---|---|---|---|

| Shared-Workspace | Agents read/write to a common repository | Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) | Temporal and spatial decoupling | Retrieval quality degrades with scale |
| Discussion-Based | Agents engage in structured multi-round dialogue | ChatEval (Chan et al., 2023) | Improved reasoning via diverse critique | High latency and cost; convergence not guaranteed |

## 6. Research Gap and Future Research Direction

**Identified gap:** No existing pattern provides a principled mechanism for *communication cost budgeting* under token constraints. Li et al. **Proposed future direction:** Develop a *meta-communication protocol* where agents negotiate a communication budget before each interaction round. Agents would bid for the right to speak or write based on the estimated value of their contribution, and a lightweight arbiter would allocate tokens to the highest-value exchanges. This approach could be evaluated by measuring task accuracy per total token cost across the three communication patterns, establishing a new efficiency metric for the field.

## 1. Field Overview

Multi-agent LLM systems coordinate multiple language model instances to solve complex tasks that exceed the capability of a single agent. Communication is the backbone of such coordination, enabling agents to share information, negotiate plans, and converge on solutions. The central research problems include: (1) designing communication protocols that balance expressiveness with computational cost, (2) ensuring information fidelity as messages propagate, (3) managing agent autonomy versus alignment, and (4) scaling communication to large agent teams without combinatorial explosion. Three high-level patterns have emerged: direct message-passing, shared-workspace communication, and discussion-based communication.

## 2. Direct or Message-Passing Communication

**General approach:** Agents send structured messages to specific recipients, either point-to-point or via broadcast. Messages typically contain natural language instructions, data payloads, or queries. The topology may be fixed (e.g., a pipeline) or dynamic (agents discover peers).

**Literature example:** CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) implements role-playing communication between two LLM agents—an AI assistant and a task-specifier—that exchange messages in a turn-based loop to accomplish a user-defined goal. Each agent maintains a conversation history and appends new messages, forming a directed communication channel.

**Main strength:** High precision and traceability. Each message has a clear sender, recipient, and content, making it straightforward to audit agent interactions and attribute errors.

**Main limitation:** Poor scalability. In a team of *n* agents, naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round, and agents must maintain separate context windows for each interlocutor, quickly exhausting token budgets.

## 3. Shared-Workspace Communication

**General approach:** Agents read from and write to a common repository—a shared memory, blackboard, or vector database—rather than addressing messages to specific peers. The workspace may be structured (e.g., key-value store) or unstructured (e.g., a shared text buffer).

**Literature example:** The "Generative Agents" architecture (Park et al., 2023) uses a shared memory stream where each agent records observations and reflections. Agents retrieve relevant memories from the shared store when planning actions, enabling emergent social behaviors without direct messaging.

**Main strength:** Decouples agents in time and space. An agent can contribute information without knowing who will consume it, and consumers can retrieve information asynchronously, supporting heterogeneous agent schedules.

**Main limitation:** Information relevance and retrieval quality become bottlenecks. As the workspace grows, agents may retrieve stale, contradictory, or irrelevant entries, and there is no built-in mechanism to resolve conflicts or prioritize authoritative contributions.

## 4. Discussion-Based Communication

**General approach:** Agents engage in structured dialogue—round-robin debate, iterative critique, or moderated discussion—to converge on a shared answer or decision. This pattern often involves multiple rounds of proposal, feedback, and revision.

**Literature example:** The "ChatEval" framework (Chan et al., 2023) has multiple LLM agents扮演 different roles (e.g., debater, critic, judge) who discuss and critique each other's reasoning over several rounds before producing a final evaluation. The discussion protocol enforces turn-taking and requires agents to reference prior statements.

**Main strength:** Improves reasoning quality through diverse perspectives and self-correction. Agents exposed to counterarguments often produce more nuanced and accurate outputs than any single agent would alone.

**Main limitation:** High latency and cost. Each round multiplies token usage by the number of agents, and discussions can degenerate into echo chambers or adversarial loops if not carefully moderated. There is no guarantee of convergence to a correct answer.

## 5. Comparison Table

| Method Category | General Approach | Literature Example | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct / Message-Passing | Agents send addressed messages to specific peers | CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) | High traceability and auditability | O(n²) scaling; token exhaustion |
| Shared-Workspace | Agents read/write to a common repository | Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) | Temporal and spatial decoupling | Retrieval quality degrades with scale |
| Discussion-Based | Agents engage in structured multi-round dialogue | ChatEval (Chan et al., 2023) | Improved reasoning via diverse critique | High latency and cost; convergence not guaranteed |

## 6. Research Gap and Future Research Direction

**Identified gap:** No existing pattern provides a principled mechanism for *communication cost budgeting* under token constraints. Li et al. (2023) note that CAMEL's message-passing approach exhausts context windows after a few rounds, limiting task complexity. Park et al. (2023) observe that their shared-memory agents retrieve increasingly irrelevant memories as the simulation progresses, wasting tokens on low-value information. Both limitations point to the same underlying problem: current communication patterns lack adaptive resource allocation that respects finite token budgets while maintaining task performance.

**Why it matters:** As multi-agent systems scale to dozens or hundreds of agents for real-world applications (e.g., software engineering, scientific discovery), token costs become prohibitive. Without cost-aware communication, these systems are not economically viable for production use.

**Proposed future direction:** Develop a *meta-communication protocol* where agents negotiate a communication budget before each interaction round. Agents would bid for the right to speak or write based on the estimated value of their contribution, and a lightweight arbiter would allocate tokens to the highest-value exchanges. This approach could be evaluated by measuring task accuracy per total token cost across the three communication patterns, establishing a new efficiency metric for the field.

## 7. Conclusion

Direct message-passing offers traceability but struggles with scale; shared workspaces provide flexibility but suffer from retrieval degradation; discussion-based methods improve reasoning but incur high costs. The unifying challenge is managing communication under token constraints—a problem that, if solved, could unlock practical deployment of large-scale multi-agent LLM systems.

## 1. Field Overview

Multi-agent LLM systems coordinate multiple language model instances to solve complex tasks that exceed the capability of a single agent. Communication is the backbone of such coordination, enabling agents to share information, negotiate plans, and converge on solutions. The central research problems include: (1) designing communication protocols that balance expressiveness with computational cost, (2) ensuring information fidelity as messages propagate, (3) managing agent autonomy versus alignment, and (4) scaling communication to large agent teams without combinatorial explosion. Three high-level patterns have emerged: direct message-passing, shared-workspace communication, and discussion-based communication.

## 2. Direct or Message-Passing Communication

**General approach:** Agents send structured messages to specific recipients, either point-to-point or via broadcast. Messages typically contain natural language instructions, data payloads, or queries. The topology may be fixed (e.g., a pipeline) or dynamic (agents discover peers).

**Literature example:** CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) implements role-playing communication between two LLM agents—an AI assistant and a task-specifier—that exchange messages in a turn-based loop to accomplish a user-defined goal. Each agent maintains a conversation history and appends new messages, forming a directed communication channel.

**Main strength:** High precision and traceability. Each message has a clear sender, recipient, and content, making it straightforward to audit agent interactions and attribute errors.

**Main limitation:** Poor scalability. In a team of *n* agents, naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round, and agents must maintain separate context windows for each interlocutor, quickly exhausting token budgets.

## 3. Shared-Workspace Communication

**General approach:** Agents read from and write to a common repository—a shared memory, blackboard, or vector database—rather than addressing messages to specific peers. The workspace may be structured (e.g., key-value store) or unstructured (e.g., a shared text buffer).

**Literature example:** The "Generative Agents" architecture (Park et al., 2023) uses a shared memory stream where each agent records observations and reflections. Agents retrieve relevant memories from the shared store when planning actions, enabling emergent social behaviors without direct messaging.

**Main strength:** Decouples agents in time and space. An agent can contribute information without knowing who will consume it, and consumers can retrieve information asynchronously, supporting heterogeneous agent schedules.

**Main limitation:** Information relevance and retrieval quality become bottlenecks. As the workspace grows, agents may retrieve stale, contradictory, or irrelevant entries, and there is no built-in mechanism to resolve conflicts or prioritize authoritative contributions.

## 4. Discussion-Based Communication

**General approach:** Agents engage in structured dialogue—round-robin debate, iterative critique, or moderated discussion—to converge on a shared answer or decision. This pattern often involves multiple rounds of proposal, feedback, and revision.

**Literature example:** The "ChatEval" framework (Chan et al., 2023) has multiple LLM agents扮演 different roles (e.g., debater, critic, judge) who discuss and critique each other's reasoning over several rounds before producing a final evaluation. The discussion protocol enforces turn-taking and requires agents to reference prior statements.

**Main strength:** Improves reasoning quality through diverse perspectives and self-correction. Agents exposed to counterarguments often produce more nuanced and accurate outputs than any single agent would alone.

**Main limitation:** High latency and cost. Each round multiplies token usage by the number of agents, and discussions can degenerate into echo chambers or adversarial loops if not carefully moderated. There is no guarantee of convergence to a correct answer.

## 5. Comparison Table

| Method Category | General Approach | Literature Example | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct / Message-Passing | Agents send addressed messages to specific peers | CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) | High traceability and auditability | O(n²) scaling; token exhaustion |
| Shared-Workspace | Agents read/write to a common repository | Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) | Temporal and spatial decoupling | Retrieval quality degrades with scale |
| Discussion-Based | Agents engage in structured multi-round dialogue | ChatEval (Chan et al., 2023) | Improved reasoning via diverse critique | High latency and cost; convergence not guaranteed |

## 6. Research Gap and Future Research Direction

**Identified gap:** No existing pattern provides a principled mechanism for *communication cost budgeting* under token constraints. Li et al. (2023) note that CAMEL's message-passing approach exhausts context windows after a few rounds, limiting task complexity. Park et al. (2023) observe that their shared-memory agents retrieve increasingly irrelevant memories as the simulation progresses, wasting tokens on low-value information. Both limitations point to the same underlying problem: current communication patterns lack adaptive resource allocation that respects finite token budgets while maintaining task performance.

**Why it matters:** As multi-agent systems scale to dozens or hundreds of agents for real-world applications (e.g., software engineering, scientific discovery), token costs become prohibitive. Without cost-aware communication, these systems are not economically viable for production use.

**Proposed future direction:** Develop a *meta-communication protocol* where agents negotiate a communication budget before each interaction round. Agents would bid for the right to speak or write based on the estimated value of their contribution, and a lightweight arbiter would allocate tokens to the highest-value exchanges. This approach could be evaluated by measuring task accuracy per total token cost across the three communication patterns, establishing a new efficiency metric for the field.

## 7. Conclusion

Direct message-passing offers traceability but struggles with scale; shared workspaces provide flexibility but suffer from retrieval degradation; discussion-based methods improve reasoning but incur high costs. The unifying challenge is managing communication under token constraints—a problem that, if solved, could unlock practical deployment of large-scale multi-agent LLM systems.

## 1. Field Overview

Multi-agent LLM systems coordinate multiple language model instances to solve complex tasks that exceed the capability of a single agent. Communication is the backbone of such coordination, enabling agents to share information, negotiate plans, and converge on solutions. The central research problems include: (1) designing communication protocols that balance expressiveness with computational cost, (2) ensuring information fidelity as messages propagate, (3) managing agent autonomy versus alignment, and (4) scaling communication to large agent teams without combinatorial explosion. Three high-level patterns have emerged: direct message-passing, shared-workspace communication, and discussion-based communication.

## 2. Direct or Message-Passing Communication

**General approach:** Agents send structured messages to specific recipients, either point-to-point or via broadcast. Messages typically contain natural language instructions, data payloads, or queries. The topology may be fixed (e.g., a pipeline) or dynamic (agents discover peers).

**Literature example:** CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) implements role-playing communication between two LLM agents—an AI assistant and a task-specifier—that exchange messages in a turn-based loop to accomplish a user-defined goal. Each agent maintains a conversation history and appends new messages, forming a directed communication channel.

**Main strength:** High precision and traceability. Each message has a clear sender, recipient, and content, making it straightforward to audit agent interactions and attribute errors.

**Main limitation:** Poor scalability. In a team of *n* agents, naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round, and agents must maintain separate context windows for each interlocutor, quickly exhausting token budgets.

## 3. Shared-Workspace Communication

**General approach:** Agents read from and write to a common repository—a shared memory, blackboard, or vector database—rather than addressing messages to specific peers. The workspace may be structured (e.g., key-value store) or unstructured (e.g., a shared text buffer).

**Literature example:** The "Generative Agents" architecture (Park et al., 2023) uses a shared memory stream where each agent records observations and reflections. Agents retrieve relevant memories from the shared store when planning actions, enabling emergent social behaviors without direct messaging.

**Main strength:** Decouples agents in time and space. An agent can contribute information without knowing who will consume it, and consumers can retrieve information asynchronously, supporting heterogeneous agent schedules.

**Main limitation:** Information relevance and retrieval quality become bottlenecks. As the workspace grows, agents may retrieve stale, contradictory, or irrelevant entries, and there is no built-in mechanism to resolve conflicts or prioritize authoritative contributions.

## 4. Discussion-Based Communication

**General approach:** Agents engage in structured dialogue—round-robin debate, iterative critique, or moderated discussion—to converge on a shared answer or decision. This pattern often involves multiple rounds of proposal, feedback, and revision.

**Literature example:** The "ChatEval" framework (Chan et al., 2023) has multiple LLM agents扮演 different roles (e.g., debater, critic, judge) who discuss and critique each other's reasoning over several rounds before producing a final evaluation. The discussion protocol enforces turn-taking and requires agents to reference prior statements.

**Main strength:** Improves reasoning quality through diverse perspectives and self-correction. Agents exposed to counterarguments often produce more nuanced and accurate outputs than any single agent would alone.

**Main limitation:** High latency and cost. Each round multiplies token usage by the number of agents, and discussions can degenerate into echo chambers or adversarial loops if not carefully moderated. There is no guarantee of convergence to a correct answer.

## 5. Comparison Table

| Method Category | General Approach | Literature Example | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct / Message-Passing | Agents send addressed messages to specific peers | CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) | High traceability and auditability | O(n²) scaling; token exhaustion |
| Shared-Workspace | Agents read/write to a common repository | Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) | Temporal and spatial decoupling | Retrieval quality degrades with scale |
| Discussion-Based | Agents engage in structured multi-round dialogue | ChatEval (Chan et al., 2023) | Improved reasoning via diverse critique | High latency and cost; convergence not guaranteed |

## 6. Research Gap and Future Research Direction

**Identified gap:** No existing pattern provides a principled mechanism for *communication cost budgeting* under token constraints. Li et al. (2023) note that CAMEL's message-passing approach exhausts context windows after a few rounds, limiting task complexity. Park et al. (2023) observe that their shared-memory agents retrieve increasingly irrelevant memories as the simulation progresses, wasting tokens on low-value information. Both limitations point to the same underlying problem: current communication patterns lack adaptive resource allocation that respects finite token budgets while maintaining task performance.

**Why it matters:** As multi-agent systems scale to dozens or hundreds of agents for real-world applications (e.g., software engineering, scientific discovery), token costs become prohibitive. Without cost-aware communication, these systems are not economically viable for production use.

**Proposed future direction:** Develop a *meta-communication protocol* where agents negotiate a communication budget before each interaction round. Agents would bid for the right to speak or write based on the estimated value of their contribution, and a lightweight arbiter would allocate tokens to the highest-value exchanges. This approach could be evaluated by measuring task accuracy per total token cost across the three communication patterns, establishing a new efficiency metric for the field.

## 7. Conclusion

Direct message-passing offers traceability but struggles with scale; shared workspaces provide flexibility but suffer from retrieval degradation; discussion-based methods improve reasoning but incur high costs. The unifying challenge is managing communication under token constraints—a problem that, if solved, could unlock practical deployment of large-scale multi-agent LLM systems.

## 1. Field Overview

Multi-agent LLM systems coordinate multiple language model instances to solve complex tasks that exceed the capability of a single agent. Communication is the backbone of such coordination, enabling agents to share information, negotiate plans, and converge on solutions. The central research problems include: (1) designing communication protocols that balance expressiveness with computational cost, (2) ensuring information fidelity as messages propagate, (3) managing agent autonomy versus alignment, and (4) scaling communication to large agent teams without combinatorial explosion. Three high-level patterns have emerged: direct message-passing, shared-workspace communication, and discussion-based communication.

## 2. Direct or Message-Passing Communication

**General approach:** Agents send structured messages to specific recipients, either point-to-point or via broadcast. Messages typically contain natural language instructions, data payloads, or queries. The topology may be fixed (e.g., a pipeline) or dynamic (agents discover peers).

**Literature example:** CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) implements role-playing communication between two LLM agents—an AI assistant and a task-specifier—that exchange messages in a turn-based loop to accomplish a user-defined goal. Each agent maintains a conversation history and appends new messages, forming a directed communication channel.

**Main strength:** High precision and traceability. Each message has a clear sender, recipient, and content, making it straightforward to audit agent interactions and attribute errors.

**Main limitation:** Poor scalability. In a team of *n* agents, naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round, and agents must maintain separate context windows for each interlocutor, quickly exhausting token budgets.

## 3. Shared-Workspace Communication

**General approach:** Agents read from and write to a common repository—a shared memory, blackboard, or vector database—rather than addressing messages to specific peers. The workspace may be structured (e.g., key-value store) or unstructured (e.g., a shared text buffer).

**Literature example:** The "Generative Agents" architecture (Park et al., 2023) uses a shared memory stream where each agent records observations and reflections. Agents retrieve relevant memories from the shared store when planning actions, enabling emergent social behaviors without direct messaging.

**Main strength:** Decouples agents in time and space. An agent can contribute information without knowing who will consume it, and consumers can retrieve information asynchronously, supporting heterogeneous agent schedules.

**Main limitation:** Information relevance and retrieval quality become bottlenecks. As the workspace grows, agents may retrieve stale, contradictory, or irrelevant entries, and there is no built-in mechanism to resolve conflicts or prioritize authoritative contributions.

## 4. Discussion-Based Communication

**General approach:** Agents engage in structured dialogue—round-robin debate, iterative critique, or moderated discussion—to converge on a shared answer or decision. This pattern often involves multiple rounds of proposal, feedback, and revision.

**Literature example:** The "ChatEval" framework (Chan et al., 2023) has multiple LLM agents扮演 different roles (e.g., debater, critic, judge) who discuss and critique each other's reasoning over several rounds before producing a final evaluation. The discussion protocol enforces turn-taking and requires agents to reference prior statements.

**Main strength:** Improves reasoning quality through diverse perspectives and self-correction. Agents exposed to counterarguments often produce more nuanced and accurate outputs than any single agent would alone.

**Main limitation:** High latency and cost. Each round multiplies token usage by the number of agents, and discussions can degenerate into echo chambers or adversarial loops if not carefully moderated. There is no guarantee of convergence to a correct answer.

## 5. Comparison Table

| Method Category | General Approach | Literature Example | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct / Message-Passing | Agents send addressed messages to specific peers | CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) | High traceability and auditability | O(n²) scaling; token exhaustion |
| Shared-Workspace | Agents read/write to a common repository | Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) | Temporal and spatial decoupling | Retrieval quality degrades with scale |
| Discussion-Based | Agents engage in structured multi-round dialogue | ChatEval (Chan et al., 2023) | Improved reasoning via diverse critique | High latency and cost; convergence not guaranteed |

## 6. Research Gap and Future Research Direction

**Identified gap:** No existing pattern provides a principled mechanism for *communication cost budgeting* under token constraints. Li et al. (2023) note that CAMEL's message-passing approach exhausts context windows after a few rounds, limiting task complexity. Park et al. (2023) observe that their shared-memory agents retrieve increasingly irrelevant memories as the simulation progresses, wasting tokens on low-value information. Both limitations point to the same underlying problem: current communication patterns lack adaptive resource allocation that respects finite token budgets while maintaining task performance.

**Why it matters:** As multi-agent systems scale to dozens or hundreds of agents for real-world applications (e.g., software engineering, scientific discovery), token costs become prohibitive. Without cost-aware communication, these systems are not economically viable for production use.

**Proposed future direction:** Develop a *meta-communication protocol* where agents negotiate a communication budget before each interaction round. Agents would bid for the right to speak or write based on the estimated value of their contribution, and a lightweight arbiter would allocate tokens to the highest-value exchanges. This approach could be evaluated by measuring task accuracy per total token cost across the three communication patterns, establishing a new efficiency metric for the field.

## 7. Conclusion

Direct message-passing offers traceability but struggles with scale; shared workspaces provide flexibility but suffer from retrieval degradation; discussion-based methods improve reasoning but incur high costs. The unifying challenge is managing communication under token constraints—a problem that, if solved, could unlock practical deployment of large-scale multi-agent LLM systems.

## 1. Field Overview

Multi-agent LLM systems coordinate multiple language model instances to solve complex tasks that exceed the capability of a single agent. Communication is the backbone of such coordination, enabling agents to share information, negotiate plans, and converge on solutions. The central research problems include: (1) designing communication protocols that balance expressiveness with computational cost, (2) ensuring information fidelity as messages propagate, (3) managing agent autonomy versus alignment, and (4) scaling communication to large agent teams without combinatorial explosion. Three high-level patterns have emerged: direct message-passing, shared-workspace communication, and discussion-based communication.

## 2. Direct or Message-Passing Communication

**General approach:** Agents send structured messages to specific recipients, either point-to-point or via broadcast. Messages typically contain natural language instructions, data payloads, or queries. The topology may be fixed (e.g., a pipeline) or dynamic (agents discover peers).

**Literature example:** CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) implements role-playing communication between two LLM agents—an AI assistant and a task-specifier—that exchange messages in a turn-based loop to accomplish a user-defined goal. Each agent maintains a conversation history and appends new messages, forming a directed communication channel.

**Main strength:** High precision and traceability. Each message has a clear sender, recipient, and content, making it straightforward to audit agent interactions and attribute errors.

**Main limitation:** Poor scalability. In a team of *n* agents, naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round, and agents must maintain separate context windows for each interlocutor, quickly exhausting token budgets.

## 3. Shared-Workspace Communication

**General approach:** Agents read from and write to a common repository—a shared memory, blackboard, or vector database—rather than addressing messages to specific peers. The workspace may be structured (e.g., key-value store) or unstructured (e.g., a shared text buffer).

**Literature example:** The "Generative Agents" architecture (Park et al., 2023) uses a shared memory stream where each agent records observations and reflections. Agents retrieve relevant memories from the shared store when planning actions, enabling emergent social behaviors without direct messaging.

**Main strength:** Decouples agents in time and space. An agent can contribute information without knowing who will consume it, and consumers can retrieve information asynchronously, supporting heterogeneous agent schedules.

**Main limitation:** Information relevance and retrieval quality become bottlenecks. As the workspace grows, agents may retrieve stale, contradictory, or irrelevant entries, and there is no built-in mechanism to resolve conflicts or prioritize authoritative contributions.

## 4. Discussion-Based Communication

**General approach:** Agents engage in structured dialogue—round-robin debate, iterative critique, or moderated discussion—to converge on a shared answer or decision. This pattern often involves multiple rounds of proposal, feedback, and revision.

**Literature example:** The "ChatEval" framework (Chan et al., 2023) has multiple LLM agents扮演 different roles (e.g., debater, critic, judge) who discuss and critique each other's reasoning over several rounds before producing a final evaluation. The discussion protocol enforces turn-taking and requires agents to reference prior statements.

**Main strength:** Improves reasoning quality through diverse perspectives and self-correction. Agents exposed to counterarguments often produce more nuanced and accurate outputs than any single agent would alone.

**Main limitation:** High latency and cost. Each round multiplies token usage by the number of agents, and discussions can degenerate into echo chambers or adversarial loops if not carefully moderated. There is no guarantee of convergence to a correct answer.

## 5. Comparison Table

| Method Category | General Approach | Literature Example | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct / Message-Passing | Agents send addressed messages to specific peers | CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) | High traceability and auditability | O(n²) scaling; token exhaustion |
| Shared-Workspace | Agents read/write to a common repository | Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) | Temporal and spatial decoupling | Retrieval quality degrades with scale |
| Discussion-Based | Agents engage in structured multi-round dialogue | ChatEval (Chan et al., 2023) | Improved reasoning via diverse critique | High latency and cost; convergence not guaranteed |

## 6. Research Gap and Future Research Direction

**Identified gap:** No existing pattern provides a principled mechanism for *communication cost budgeting* under token constraints. Li et al. (2023) note that CAMEL's message-passing approach exhausts context windows after a few rounds, limiting task complexity. Park et al. (2023) observe that their shared-memory agents retrieve increasingly irrelevant memories as the simulation progresses, wasting tokens on low-value information. Both limitations point to the same underlying problem: current communication patterns lack adaptive resource allocation that respects finite token budgets while maintaining task performance.

**Why it matters:** As multi-agent systems scale to dozens or hundreds of agents for real-world applications (e.g., software engineering, scientific discovery), token costs become prohibitive. Without cost-aware communication, these systems are not economically viable for production use.

**Proposed future direction:** Develop a *meta-communication protocol* where agents negotiate a communication budget before each interaction round. Agents would bid for the right to speak or write based on the estimated value of their contribution, and a lightweight arbiter would allocate tokens to the highest-value exchanges. This approach could be evaluated by measuring task accuracy per total token cost across the three communication patterns, establishing a new efficiency metric for the field.

## 7. Conclusion

Direct message-passing offers traceability but struggles with scale; shared workspaces provide flexibility but suffer from retrieval degradation; discussion-based methods improve reasoning but incur high costs. The unifying challenge is managing communication under token constraints—a problem that, if solved, could unlock practical deployment of large-scale multi-agent LLM systems.

final_output:
*Note: All academic search and lookup tools returned rate-limit or timeout errors. The citations below are based on the author's knowledge of published, peer-reviewed or widely-cited preprints. Readers should verify specific claims against the original papers.*

## 1. Field Overview

Multi-agent LLM systems coordinate multiple language model instances to solve complex tasks that exceed the capability of a single agent. Communication is the backbone of such coordination, enabling agents to share information, negotiate plans, and converge on solutions. The central research problems include: (1) designing communication protocols that balance expressiveness with computational cost, (2) ensuring information fidelity as messages propagate, (3) managing agent autonomy versus alignment, and (4) scaling communication to large agent teams without combinatorial explosion. Three high-level patterns have emerged: direct message-passing, shared-workspace communication, and discussion-based communication.

## 2. Direct or Message-Passing Communication

**General approach:** Agents send structured messages to specific recipients, either point-to-point or via broadcast. Messages typically contain natural language instructions, data payloads, or queries. The topology may be fixed (e.g., a pipeline) or dynamic (agents discover peers).

**Literature example:** CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) implements role-playing communication between two LLM agents—an AI assistant and a task-specifier—that exchange messages in a turn-based loop to accomplish a user-defined goal. Each agent maintains a conversation history and appends new messages, forming a directed communication channel.

**Main strength:** High precision and traceability. Each message has a clear sender, recipient, and content, making it straightforward to audit agent interactions and attribute errors.

**Main limitation:** Poor scalability. In a fully connected topology, naive all-to-all messaging yields O(n²) messages per round, and agents must maintain separate context windows for each interlocutor. Li et al. (2023) note that context window limits constrain the number of message exchanges possible, limiting task complexity.

## 3. Shared-Workspace Communication

**General approach:** Agents read from and write to a common repository—a shared memory, blackboard, or vector database—rather than addressing messages to specific peers. The workspace may be structured (e.g., key-value store) or unstructured (e.g., a shared text buffer).

**Literature example:** The "Generative Agents" architecture (Park et al., 2023) uses a shared memory stream where each agent records observations and reflections. Agents retrieve relevant memories from the shared store when planning actions, enabling emergent social behaviors without direct messaging.

**Main strength:** Decouples agents in time and space. An agent can contribute information without knowing who will consume it, and consumers can retrieve information asynchronously, supporting heterogeneous agent schedules.

**Main limitation:** Information relevance and retrieval quality become bottlenecks. Park et al. (2023) identify memory retrieval quality as an open challenge, particularly as the memory stream grows. There is no built-in mechanism to resolve conflicts or prioritize authoritative contributions.

## 4. Discussion-Based Communication

**General approach:** Agents engage in structured dialogue—round-robin debate, iterative critique, or moderated discussion—to converge on a shared answer or decision. This pattern often involves multiple rounds of proposal, feedback, and revision.

**Literature example:** The "ChatEval" framework (Chan et al., 2023) has multiple LLM agents扮演 different roles (e.g., debater, critic, judge) who discuss and critique each other's reasoning over several rounds before producing a final evaluation. The discussion protocol enforces turn-taking and requires agents to reference prior statements.

**Main strength:** Improves reasoning quality through diverse perspectives and self-correction. While ChatEval shows accuracy improvements on several benchmarks, agents exposed to counterarguments often produce more nuanced and accurate outputs than any single agent would alone.

**Main limitation:** High latency and cost. Each round multiplies token usage by the number of agents, and discussions can degenerate into echo chambers or adversarial loops if not carefully moderated. The framework does not guarantee convergence to a correct answer, and performance varies by task.

## 5. Comparison Table

| Method Category | General Approach | Literature Example | Main Strength | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct / Message-Passing | Agents send addressed messages to specific peers | CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) | High traceability and auditability | O(n²) scaling in fully connected topologies; token exhaustion |
| Shared-Workspace | Agents read/write to a common repository | Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) | Temporal and spatial decoupling | Retrieval quality degrades as memory stream grows |
| Discussion-Based | Agents engage in structured multi-round dialogue | ChatEval (Chan et al., 2023) | Improved reasoning via diverse critique | High latency and cost; convergence not guaranteed |

## 6. Research Gap and Future Research Direction

**Identified gap:** No existing pattern provides a principled mechanism for *communication cost budgeting* under token constraints. Neither Li et al. (2023) nor Park et al. (2023) explicitly identify this as a research gap, but both papers point to related token-efficiency limitations that, taken together, motivate this direction. Li et al. (2023) note that context window limits constrain message exchanges in direct communication, while Park et al. (2023) identify memory retrieval quality as an open challenge as the shared workspace grows. Both limitations stem from the same underlying problem: current communication patterns lack adaptive resource allocation that respects finite token budgets while maintaining task performance.

**Why it matters:** As multi-agent systems scale to dozens or hundreds of agents for real-world applications (e.g., software engineering, scientific discovery), token costs become prohibitive. Without cost-aware communication, these systems are not economically viable for production use.

**Proposed future direction:** Develop a *meta-communication protocol* where agents negotiate a communication budget before each interaction round. Agents would bid for the right to speak or write based on the estimated value of their contribution, and a lightweight arbiter would allocate tokens to the highest-value exchanges. This approach could be evaluated by measuring task accuracy per total token cost across the three communication patterns, establishing a new efficiency metric for the field.

## 7. Conclusion

Direct message-passing offers traceability but struggles with scale; shared workspaces provide flexibility but suffer from retrieval degradation; discussion-based methods improve reasoning but incur high costs. The unifying challenge is managing communication under token constraints—a problem that, if solved, could unlock practical deployment of large-scale multi-agent LLM systems.

## 8. References

Chan, C.-M., Chen, W., Su, Y., Yu, J., Xue, W., Zhang, Y., Fu, J., & Liu, Z. (2023). ChatEval: Towards Better LLM-based Evaluators through Multi-Agent Debate. *arXiv preprint arXiv:2308.07201*.

Li, G., Hammoud, H., Itani, H., Khizbullin, D., & Ghanem, B. (2023). CAMEL: Communicative Agents for "Mind" Exploration of Large Language Model Society. *Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems*, 36.

Park, J. S., O'Brien, J. C., Cai, C. J., Morris, M. R., Liang, P., & Bernstein, M. S. (2023). Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior. *Proceedings of the 36th Annual ACM Symposium on User Interface Software and Technology (UIST)*.

agent_task.prompt: Write a 500-800 word literature review on communication methods in multi-agent LLM systems. Briefly summarize the purpose of communication in multi-agent LLM systems and the main research problems addressed in this field. Then review and compare the following three high-level communication patterns: (1) direct or message-passing communication, (2) shared-workspace communication such as shared memory or blackboard systems, and (3) discussion-based communication such as group discussion, debate, or iterative critique. Real systems may combine more than one pattern. For each category, explain the general approach, provide at least one relevant literature example, and identify one main strength and one main limitation. Include a comparison table with the columns: Method Category, General Approach, Literature Example, Main Strength, and Main Limitation. Finally, identify one specific unresolved issue or research gap supported by limitations discussed in at least two reviewed sources, explain why it matters, and propose one related future research direction. Cite at least three reliable sources, including at least two academic papers, conference papers, journal articles, or recognized research preprints. Use in-text citations that correspond to the reference list.
